text: A reusable D port is introduced into the anus, with the tumor located 5.5 centimeters from the anal margin.

A butterfly needle is inserted intraluminally, and indocyanine green (ICG) is injected peritumorally at the nine, twelve, and three o'clock positions. Additional injections are made into the rectal mucosa and tumor, followed by the removal of the butterfly needle.
The laparoscopic view reveals ICG fluorescence diffusion around the rectum and along the lymphatic vessels. The surgeon performs vascular dissection using laparoscopic instruments.

Monocurved reusable instruments are inserted into the transanal D-port. The surgeon initiates transanal minimally invasive surgery by placing a purse-string suture one centimeter below the tumor using submucosal bites and PDS-II zero suture material. An extracorporeal knot is tied, closing the lumen by pushing it.

A monocurved coagulating hook is inserted to perform mucosal dissection, marked with a tattoo. The surgeon initiates a full-thickness rectal wall perforation starting posteriorly, with enhanced visibility due to pneumo-retrorectum. ICG fluorescence appears as dissection progresses using monocurved grasping forceps and the coagulating hook, moving from posteriorly to laterally and anteriorly.

The surgeon uses ICG fluorescence to reveal and dissect the lower part of the recto-sacral ligament until the pre-sacral fascia is identified. The pre-sacral fascia is carefully separated from the visceral rectal fascia and held inferiorly. Fluorescence confirms the presence of ICG above the dissected plane. The dissection proceeds with the mesorectum being liberated from the pre-sacral fascia, continuing posteriorly and laterally on both sides.

The surgeon performs anterior dissection, freeing the rectum from the vagina within the rectovaginal septum. The ICG fluorescence is visible in the area, and the vagina is gently pushed anteriorly. The dissection proceeds laterally, with careful attention to avoid injuring the splanchnic nerves.

The surgeon isolates, clips, and divides the right inferior rectal artery. The dissection proceeds anteriorly until the abdominal scope is visible, and posteriorly, it concludes at the level of the promontory.

The surgeon maintains the rectum and periadipous tissue in a downward position, guided by the fluorescent plane of dissection. Anteriorly, the peritoneal sheet is perforated, reaching Douglas' pouch. Subsequent perforations are made at the promontory and laterally on both sides.

The final view of the total mesorectal excision (TME) is displayed. A plastic protection is inserted into the D-port, and the rectum is placed inside this protection. The D-port is removed while holding the specimen with a monocurve grasper. The specimen is then extracted transanally, with the orifice protected by the plastic covering.

The surgeon cuts the descending colon and introduces a circular anvil into the colic lumen, pushing it into the pelvis. The D-port is repositioned, and the rectal stump is closed with a PDS-II-Zero running suture. A purse-string suture is performed with four thickness bites under transanal and laparoscopic scope control, utilizing curved instruments for ergonomic efficiency.

The monocurved anvil grasping forceps hold the spike of the anvil centered in the rectal stump as the extracorporeal knot is tied. The rectal stump is closed around the anvil spike, and the suture is cut. The D-port is removed, and the circular stapler is inserted and connected to the anvil. The monocurved anvil grasper is removed, and the anastomosis is performed under abdominal laparoscopic control. A final endoluminal view of the completed anastomosis is shown.
